mainEntity.name: How much does a Range Rover Sport 2.7 TDV6 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 2.7 TDV6 engine typically costs £5,000–£7,500 supply only. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,500 depending on the specialist. Used engines can be found from £2,800–£4,500, but condition varies dramatically and the oil pump housing risk applies to all used units.

mainEntity.name: Is the Range Rover Sport 2.7 TDV6 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 276DT is a capable engine let down by one critical design flaw — the oil pump housing. With the housing preventatively replaced, the 2.7 TDV6 is a robust, refined V6 diesel. Without it, you're at risk of sudden, catastrophic failure. Cold-start knock is the early warning sign that should never be ignored.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Range Rover Sport 2.7 TDV6?

acceptedAnswer.text: The main issue is oil pump housing failure (the housing can fracture at the tensioner mount, causing sudden oil pressure loss and engine destruction). Other issues include crankshaft/bearing failure (often secondary to oil pump failure), EGR valve and intake manifold carbon buildup, and occasional turbocharger issues.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a 2.7 TDV6 engine, or should I scrap the vehicle?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the vehicle's condition. Against a well-maintained L320 worth £5,000–£8,000, a £6,000–£7,000 replacement may be 75–100% of the vehicle's value — borderline but often worth it for a cherished example. Against a heavily worn example worth £3,000–£4,000, the replacement cost exceeds our 60% Land Rover threshold — get a scrap valuation first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Range Rover Sport 2.7 TDV6 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is 276DT, found on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation. It's the 2.7-litre V6 turbo-diesel fitted to the pre-facelift L320 Range Rover Sport from 2005–2009.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair a 276DT oil pump housing, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The housing must be replaced — it's a known design flaw where the original casting can fracture at the tensioner mount. A preventative replacement costs £1,500–£3,000. A failed housing almost always destroys the engine, requiring a £5,000–£7,500 engine replacement. This is non-negotiable maintenance on any 2.7 TDV6.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Range Rover Sport 2.7 TDV6 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential maintenance on any 276DT — don't wait
Bearings and crank are typically destroyed
Only if caught extremely early; specialist-only work
Viable repair on a well-maintained vehicle

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 2.7 TDV6? Check These First
buyIf: * Oil pump housing replaced (critical — confirm this work has been done)
* No cold-start knocking (listen carefully on first start)
* Full service history with regular oil changes (oil condition is critical)
* Long MOT with no emissions-related failures
avoidIf: * No record of oil pump housing work — assume it's overdue and budget £1,500–£3,000
* Cold-start knocking — the 276DT is a one-shot engine; budget for replacement
* Poor service history or extended oil change intervals — the 276DT doesn't forgive neglect
* Overheating history — may indicate head gasket or cooling system issues

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 2.7 TDV6 in the pre-facelift Range Rover Sport is a capable, torquey engine that's let down by one critical design flaw — the oil pump housing. Address this preventatively and the 276DT is a robust, refined V6 diesel. Ignore it and you're looking at a £5,000–£7,500 engine replacement. With typical L320 values of £4,000–£10,000, an engine replacement is borderline but often worth it for a cherished example. Oil pump housing work is non-negotiable on any 2.7 TDV6.

cta.label: → Compare 2.7 TDV6 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Range Rover Sport 2.7 TDV6 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Oil Pump Housing — Confirm replacement (critical — ask for proof on any 2.7 TDV6)
* Cold Start — Listen carefully for any knocking or rattle; any is a red flag
* Oil Pressure — Check for oil pressure warning light; any flicker is a serious concern
* Service History — Including regular oil changes (5,000-mile intervals preferred) — see the map above
* Smoke Check — Black smoke = EGR/over-fuelling; white smoke = coolant ingress
* Turbo Boost — Test acceleration; any lack of power or hesitation?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate EGR or injector problems
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures and structural corrosion
